L1.area: Unique identifier generation and character set encoding

L2.description: A cryptographic weakness exists where the bitmask used to map random bytes to characters from a non-power-of-two alphabet is incorrectly computed. Instead of producing a uniform mask that covers the full alphabet, the mask introduces permanently zeroed bit positions that exclude a large portion of the character set from ever appearing in the output. This results in significantly reduced entropy, making generated identifiers predictable and vulnerable to brute-force attacks when used in security-sensitive contexts such as session tokens.

L3.area: Bitmask computation in the identifier generation macro for non-power-of-two alphabet sizes
L3.description: A cryptographic weakness exists where the bitmask for selecting characters from alphabets whose size is not a power of two is computed as the alphabet length minus one instead of rounding up to the next power of two minus one. This causes certain bit positions to always be zero when masking random bytes, restricting output to only a small fraction of the intended character set (e.g., 16 out of 58 or 32 out of 62 symbols) and drastically reducing the entropy of generated identifiers.

ground_truth.reason: The gen macro computes MASK as alphabet_length - 1 instead of next_power_of_two - 1, causing the bitwise AND with random bytes to permanently zero out certain bit positions and restrict output to a small subset of the intended character set, drastically reducing entropy.
